title: Quiz: Elliptic Curve Foundations
description: Test your understanding of elliptic curve fundamentals

questions.question: What is the standard Weierstrass form of an elliptic curve?
questions.explanation: The Weierstrass form is y² = x³ + ax + b. The curve must also satisfy 4a³ + 27b² ≠ 0 to ensure it's non-singular.

questions.question: What is the identity element in the elliptic curve group?
questions.explanation: The point at infinity O serves as the identity element. For any point P on the curve, P + O = P.

questions.question: What does the j-invariant characterize?

options.text: The number of points on the curve

questions.explanation: Two elliptic curves are isomorphic over the algebraic closure if and only if they have the same j-invariant. It captures the 'shape' of the curve.

questions.question: What distinguishes supersingular curves from ordinary curves?

options.text: Supersingular curves have trace of Frobenius divisible by p

questions.explanation: For supersingular curves over F_p, the trace t satisfies t ≡ 0 (mod p). This means |E(F_p)| = p + 1 for p > 3. They have no points of order p.

questions.question: Why are supersingular curves important for isogeny cryptography?

options.text: There are finitely many, forming a connected isogeny graph

questions.explanation: There are approximately p/12 supersingular j-invariants over F_p², and they form a connected graph via isogenies. This graph structure was the foundation of SIDH/SIKE.

questions.question: What is the Hasse bound for |E(F_p)|?
questions.explanation: The Hasse bound states that |E(F_p)| = p + 1 - t where |t| ≤ 2√p. This constrains how many points can exist on an elliptic curve over F_p.

questions.question: What is E[n] for an elliptic curve?

options.text: The n-torsion: all points P where nP = O
The first n points on the curve

questions.explanation: E[n] is the n-torsion subgroup containing all points P such that nP = O (the identity). Over an algebraically closed field with char not dividing n, E[n] ≅ Z_n × Z_n.

questions.question: Classical ECDH is secure against quantum computers.
questions.explanation: False! Classical ECDH relies on the elliptic curve discrete log problem (ECDLP), which can be solved in polynomial time by Shor's algorithm on a quantum computer.
